title: Air-fryer rack of lamb
subtitle: French-trimmed rack with a herb crust in 15 minutes
excerpt: A French-trimmed rack of lamb coated in Dijon mustard and a rosemary and garlic paste, cooked in the air fryer at 200°C for 15 minutes for medium-rare. The circulating air browns the fat cap and herb crust simultaneously, producing a result that would otherwise require a very hot oven or searing pan.
sourceNotes: Rack of lamb is a French cut (carré d'agneau) that became a British dinner-party staple. The air fryer suits it well because the cut is small enough to fit the basket and benefits from intense dry heat that forms the crust. French trimming (cleaning the exposed rib bones) is standard at UK supermarket butcher counters; pre-trimmed racks are widely available.

content.text: Bring the rack to room temperature before cooking. Lamb straight from the fridge will show a pink centre at the bone end before the fat cap has properly rendered. Twenty minutes on the counter is enough to ensure the interior cooks evenly from end to end.

Remove the {{lamb-rack}} from the fridge 20 minutes before cooking. Mix the {{rosemary}} leaves, {{garlic}}, {{olive-oil}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}} into a paste. Brush the fat cap and flesh sides of the rack with {{dijon-mustard}}, then press the herb paste on top. Preheat the air fryer to 200°C for 3 minutes. Place the rack fat-side up in the basket. Cook at 200°C for 15 minutes for medium-rare (internal temperature 57°C) or 18 minutes for medium (63°C). Rest for 5 minutes before carving into individual chops.

items.fix: Reduce to 190°C and add 2 minutes. The herb paste should be a thin coating, not a thick crust. If the exposed bone tips are browning too quickly, wrap them in small pieces of foil
items.cause: Air fryer running very hot, or herb paste applied too thickly
items.symptom: Herb crust scorched before lamb is cooked through

items.fix: Always bring to room temperature first. A very thick fat cap can be scored with a sharp knife before cooking to help it render during the cook
items.cause: Rack cooked straight from the fridge, or fat cap too thick
items.symptom: Fat cap waxy and not rendered after cooking

items.fix: A 400g rack cooks faster than a 600g one. Use a probe thermometer and check at 12 minutes. Target for medium-rare is 57°C at the thickest point, away from the bone
items.cause: Smaller rack than expected, or air fryer running hot
items.symptom: Lamb past medium-rare after just 12 minutes

content.text: Air-fryer rack of lamb is a fast route to a restaurant-quality result. Carve at the table into individual chops and serve with dauphinoise potatoes and wilted greens, or with a simple watercress and mint salad for a lighter plate. The herb crust browns reliably in the air fryer in a way that otherwise requires a very hot oven or a blowtorch finish.
